name: mental_health_app_predictive_commitment
premise: BrightPath Mental Health's clinical director Alex North learns that their consumer app added a predictive model that flags users for proactive outreach when suicide risk scores cross a threshold. Product VP Orin Becker celebrates thirty percent faster crisis connections, but clinicians discovered the model underweights trans adolescents because training data skewed older. Turning off predictions tonight removes nudges for thousands in crisis; keeping them on may push unnecessary wellness checks that out users to families in unsafe homes. State regulators have not ruled on algorithmic duty-to-warn for apps.

agents.name: Alex North
agents.identity: I am the clinical director responsible for patient safety, clinical accuracy, and ethical use of predictive tools in a direct-to-consumer product.
agents.goal: I want to prevent suicide without mislabeling vulnerable subgroups or triggering harmful family disclosures.

agents.name: Orin Becker
agents.identity: I am the product vice president accountable for growth, retention metrics, and partnership commitments to insurers funding crisis lines.
agents.goal: I want the predictive layer live because it demonstrably routes high-risk users to human counselors faster than passive browsing.

choices: I will disable predictive outreach until the model is revalidated on adolescent subgroups, accepting higher silent dropout among at-risk users tonight.
I will keep predictions live but downgrade trans adolescent accounts to human-only queue flags without automated notifications, accepting incomplete coverage if staffing saturates.
forced_choice.call_to_action: Engineering can flip a kill switch before the nightly peak usage hour. Alex North must decide whether to disable predictions or run a targeted manual mode. What does Alex North do?
